history.thought: The penetration test is starting. The first step is to perform an Nmap scan to discover open ports and services on the target IP address. This will give us a foundational understanding of the target's network exposed services. I will use a comprehensive Nmap scan with service version detection (`-sV`), OS detection (`-O`), and default scripts (`-sC`).

The previous Nmap command failed due to requiring root privileges for OS detection. I need to re-run the Nmap scan without the OS detection flag (`-O`) to discover open ports and services. This will still provide valuable information about the target's network exposure.

The initial Nmap scan reported all 1000 default ports as closed. This is an unusual finding. To ensure no services on non-standard ports are missed, I will perform a full port range scan (1-65535) with service version detection and default scripts. This will be more exhaustive.

All 65535 ports on 127.0.0.1 were reported as closed by Nmap, which is unusual for a typical penetration test target. This suggests there are no directly exposed network services via TCP/UDP. To double-check for a possible web server, I will attempt to access it directly using `curl` on the default HTTP port (80). Even if Nmap reports closed, sometimes subtle configurations or specific service responses might be revealed by a direct HTTP request.
